{{Short description|Men's association football team}}
{{About|the men's team|the women's team|Cameroon women's national football team}}

| Sub-confederation        = [[Central African Football Federations' Union|UNIFFAC]] (Central Africa)
| Coach                    = [[David Pagou]] 
| Captain                  = [[Christian Bassogog]]

| Top scorer               = [[Samuel Eto'o]] ([[List of international goals scored by Samuel Eto'o|56]])<ref name="9 Samuel ETOO">{{cite web |url=https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/players/player=170667/index.html |title=9 Samuel ETOO |work=FIFA.com |access-date=1 March 2016 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140618045733/http://www.fifa.com/worldcup/players/player%3D170667/index.html |archive-date=18 June 2014 }}</ref>

| FIFA min date            = February – March 2013

| Elo min date             = April 1995

| rightarm3                = FFFFFF
| shorts3                  = FFFFFF
| socks3                   = FFFFFF
| First game               = {{nowrap|{{Flagdeco|Belgian Congo}} [[DR Congo national football team|Belgian Congo]] 3–2 '''French Cameroon''' {{flagdeco|FRA}}<br>([[Belgian Congo]]; September 1956)}}
| Largest win              = {{nowrap|{{fb|CMR|1961}} 9–0 {{fb-rt|CHA}}<br>([[Kinshasa]], [[Democratic Republic of the Congo|DR Congo]]; 7 April 1965)}}
| Largest loss             = {{fb|KOR|1984}} 5–0 {{fb-rt|CMR}}<br>([[Seoul]], [[South Korea]]; 4 October 1984)<br/>{{fb|NOR}} 6–1 {{fb-rt|CMR}}<br>([[Oslo]], [[Norway]]; 31 October 1990)<br/>{{fb|RUS}} 6–1  {{fb-rt|CMR}}<br>([[Palo Alto, California|Palo Alto]], [[United States]]; 28 June 1994)<br/>{{fb|CRC}} 5–0 {{fb-rt|CMR}}<br>([[San José, Costa Rica|San José]], [[Costa Rica]]; 9 March 1997)
| World cup apps           = 8
| World cup first          = 1982
| World cup best           = Quarter-finals ([[1990 FIFA World Cup|1990]])
| Regional name            = [[Africa Cup of Nations]]
| Regional cup apps        = 22
| Regional cup first       = [[1970 African Cup of Nations|1970]]
| Regional cup best        = '''Champions''' ([[1984 African Cup of Nations|1984]], [[1984 African Cup of Nations|1988]], [[2000 African Cup of Nations|2000]], [[2002 African Cup of Nations|2002]], [[2017 Africa Cup of Nations|2017]])
| Confederations cup apps  = 3
| Confederations cup first = 2001
| Confederations cup best  = Runners-up ([[2003 FIFA Confederations Cup|2003]])
| 2ndRegional name         = [[African Nations Championship]]
| 2ndRegional cup apps     = 4
| 2ndRegional cup first    = [[2011 African Nations Championship|2011]]
| 2ndRegional cup best     = Fourth place ([[2020 African Nations Championship|2020]])
}}

The '''Cameroon national football team''' ([[French language|French]]: ''Équipe du Cameroun de football''), also known as the '''Indomitable Lions''' (French: ''les lions indomptables''),{{efn|Most of the national sporting teams in Cameroon go by this name, including the [[Cameroon national rugby league team]].<ref name=abc2022>{{cite web | title=The last hunt of Carol Manga, rugby league's indomitable lion of Cameroon | first=Nick | last=Campton | website=ABC News | publisher=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] | date=5 September 2022 | url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2022-09-05/carol-manga-and-his-rugby-league-comeback-for-cameroon/101388698 | access-date=5 September 2022 | archive-date=4 September 2022 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220904235313/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2022-09-05/carol-manga-and-his-rugby-league-comeback-for-cameroon/101388698 | url-status=live }}</ref>}} represents [[Cameroon]] in men's international [[Association football|football]]. It is controlled by the [[Cameroonian Football Federation|Fédération Camerounaise de Football]], a member of [[FIFA]] and its African confederation [[Confederation of African Football|CAF]].

The team has qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup]] eight times, more than any other African team, and four times in a row between [[1990 FIFA World Cup|1990]] and [[2002 FIFA World Cup|2002]]. However, the team has only made it out of the group stage once. They were the first African team to reach the quarter-final of the World Cup<ref>{{Cite web |title=Cameroon In 1990, Ghana In 2010…Morocco Make It Fourth Time Lucky For Africa At W/Cup |url=https://www.channelstv.com/2022/12/10/cameroon-in-1990-ghana-in-2010-morocco-make-it-fourth-time-lucky-for-africa-at-w-cup/ |website=Channels Tv |date=10 December 2022 }}</ref> in 1990, losing to [[England national football team|England]] in extra time. They have also won five [[Africa Cup of Nations]].

Cameroon is the first and, as of 2022, only African country to win against [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]] in tournament play, defeating them in the [[2003 FIFA Confederations Cup|2003 Confederations Cup]] and [[2022 FIFA World Cup]] by identical 1–0 scores.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Mbale |first=Philemon |date=3 December 2022 |title=Qatar 2022 - Cameroon : First African team to beat Brazil in WC history |url=https://sportnewsafrica.com/en/at-a-glance/qatar-2022-cameroon-first-african-team-to-beat-brazil-in-wc-history/ |access-date=4 December 2022 |website=Sports News Africa |archive-date=4 December 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221204213023/https://sportnewsafrica.com/en/at-a-glance/qatar-2022-cameroon-first-african-team-to-beat-brazil-in-wc-history/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

In spite of a rich history, Cameroon's domestic leagues suffer from a lack of funding, compelling young players to leave prematurely for lower-tier, yet more lucrative leagues abroad. Consequently, these players are not properly developed forcing major European leagues and their clubs to look elsewhere, as they struggle to see any value in Cameroon as a pipeline, especially so with a federation management team that massively lacks in basic diplomacy.

'''Future Outlook'''

If Cameroon can manage to stabilize its governance and reinvest time and effort into local academies and pure education, the country could return to the elite level in a decade's time. Otherwise, it risks becoming merely a "historic" team - respected for its past, but lacking competitiveness in the present. <ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-06-07 |title=‘Dictator’ Samuel Eto’o and chaos in Cameroonian football |url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/football/articles/c9xxny8w745o |access-date=2026-05-30 |website=BBC Sport |language=en-GB}}</ref>

==History==

===1956–2000: early years===
Cameroon played its first match against [[DR Congo national football team|Belgian Congo]] in 1956, losing 3–2. They first qualified for the [[Africa Cup of Nations]] in [[1970 African Nations Cup|1970]], but were knocked out in the first round. Two years later, as hosts, the Indomitable Lions finished third after being knocked out by their neighbors and future champions Congo in the [[1972 Africa Cup of Nations]]. They would not qualify for the competition for another ten years.

Cameroon qualified for its first [[1982 FIFA World Cup|FIFA World Cup in 1982]]. With the increase from 16 teams to 24, Cameroon qualified along with [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]] to represent Africa at the tournament in Spain. Cameroon was drawn into Group 1 with [[Italy national football team|Italy]], [[Poland national football team|Poland]], and [[Peru national football team|Peru]]. In their first game, Cameroon faced Peru and drew 0–0. They then held Poland goalless before a surprise 1–1 draw with eventual winners Italy. Despite being unbeaten, they failed to qualify for the second round, having scored fewer goals than Italy.

Two years later, Cameroon qualified for the [[1984 Africa Cup of Nations]], held in [[Ivory Coast]]. They finished second in their first-round group before beating Algeria on penalties in the semi-final. In the final, Cameroon beat [[Nigeria national football team|Nigeria]] 3–1 with goals from [[René N'Djeya]], [[Théophile Abega]], and [[Ernest Ebongué]] to become champions of Africa for the first time.

Cameroon qualified for the [[1990 FIFA World Cup|1990 World Cup]] by surpassing Nigeria and beating [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]] in the final round playoff. In the final tournament, Cameroon were drawn into Group B with [[Argentina national football team|Argentina]], [[Romania national football team|Romania]] and the [[Soviet Union national football team|Soviet Union]]. Cameroon defeated defending champions Argentina in the opening game 1–0 with a goal scored by [[François Omam-Biyik]]. Cameroon later defeated Romania 2–1 and lost to the Soviet Union 0–4, becoming the first side to top a World Cup Finals group with a negative goal difference. In the second round, Cameroon defeated [[Colombia national football team|Colombia]] 2–1 with the 38-year-old [[Roger Milla]] scoring two goals in extra-time.

In the quarter-finals, Cameroon faced [[England national football team|England]]. After 25 minutes, England's [[David Platt (footballer)|David Platt]] scored for England, while in the second-half, Cameroon came back with a 61st-minute penalty from [[Emmanuel Kundé]] and took the lead with [[Eugène Ekéké]] on 65 minutes. England, however, equalized in the 83rd minute with a penalty from [[Gary Lineker]], while Lineker again found the net via a 105th-minute penalty to make the eventual scoreline 3–2 for England. The team was coached by Russian manager and former player [[Valeri Nepomniachi]].

[[File:Qualification_for_the_1994_FIFA_World_Cup.png|thumb|Countries qualified for the [[1994 FIFA World Cup]] are shown in dark green]]

[[File:Équipe football cameroun 01.jpg|thumb|The national football team of Cameroon]]

The [[1994 FIFA World Cup|1994 World Cup]] in the United States saw the adjustment of representation for African teams qualifying, from two to three. Cameroon qualified along with Nigeria and [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]]. In the final tournament, Cameroon were drawn into Group B with [[Sweden men's national football team|Sweden]], [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]] and [[Russia national football team|Russia]]. After a 2–2 draw against Sweden, Cameroon lost to Brazil and Russia sealed their elimination. In their last game against Russia, the then 42-year-old Roger Milla became the oldest player to play and score in a World Cup finals match. The team was coached by French-born [[Henri Michel]].
The [[1998 FIFA World Cup|1998 World Cup]] in France saw an increase of 24 to 32 teams, with Cameroon one of the five countries representing Africa. Cameroon were drawn into Group B with Italy, [[Chile national football team|Chile]] and [[Austria national football team|Austria]]. Despite drawing with Chile and Austria (after leading 1–0 against them until the 90th minute), a 3–0 defeat to Italy saw Cameroon finish bottom of the group. Cameroon had three players sent off in the course of the tournament, more than any other team. They also had the highest card count per game of any team, collecting an average of four bookings in each match they played.<ref>{{cite web | title=Top Cards – France 1998 | publisher=fifa.com | url=https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/archive/edition=1013/statistics/teams/topcards.html | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071017034127/http://fifa.com/worldcup/archive/edition=1013/statistics/teams/topcards.html | url-status=dead | archive-date=17 October 2007 | access-date=21 November 2009 }}</ref> It was also during this tournament that a certain [[Samuel Eto'o]] was exposed to Cameroonians. He was the youngest player of the tournament alongside [[Michael Owen]] of England. The team was coached by French-born [[Claude Le Roy]].

===Post-2000===

Cameroon qualified for the [[2002 FIFA World Cup|2002 World Cup]] in Korea-Japan, clinching first place in their group which included [[Angola national football team|Angola]], [[Zambia national football team|Zambia]] and [[Togo national football team|Togo]]. Cameroon were drawn into Group E alongside [[Germany national football team|Germany]], the [[Republic of Ireland national football team|Republic of Ireland]] and [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]]. Cameroon started with a 1–1 draw with Ireland after giving up the lead and later defeated Saudi Arabia 1–0. In their last game, Cameroon were defeated 2–0 by Germany and were narrowly eliminated by the Irish, who had not lost a game.

Cameroon started the [[2002 African Cup of Nations]] competition with a 1–0 win over [[DR Congo national football team|DR Congo]]. That was followed by another 1–0 win against [[Ivory Coast national football team|Ivory Coast]], and a comfortable 3–0 win against Togo. These results led Cameroon to qualify from the group stage to the quarter-finals as their group's winner. In the knockout stage, [[Patrick M'Boma|M'Boma]]'s goal in the 62nd minute lifted Cameroon over Egypt 1–0. Cameroon would defeat hosts [[Mali national football team|Mali]] 3–0 in the semi-final on 7 February on route to repeating as champions by edging Senegal 3–2 on penalties following a scoreless 120 minutes on 13 February, and thereby qualifying for the [[2003 FIFA Confederations Cup|2003 Confederations Cup]] in France.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/confederationscup/matches/round=64890600/match=64890016/photos/index.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170710164743/http://www.fifa.com/confederationscup/matches/round=64890600/match=64890016/photos/index.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=10 July 2017|title=FIFA Confederations Cup France 2003 |date=18 June 2003|website=FIFA.com|access-date=18 June 2017}}</ref>

There, the Indomitable Lions became the first African country to defeat Brazil, courtesy of Samuel Eto'o's tally in the 83rd minute of their opening match on 13 June.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|last=Mothoagae|first=Keba|date=3 December 2022|title=2022 World Cup: Brazil's Incredible Record Against African Teams Broken By Cameroon|url=https://sportsbrief.com/football/29413-2022-world-cup-brazil-record-african-teams-broken-by-cameroon/|access-date=3 December 2022|website=Sports Brief|archive-date=3 December 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221203230413/https://sportsbrief.com/football/29413-2022-world-cup-brazil-record-african-teams-broken-by-cameroon/|url-status=live}}</ref> Cameroon subsequently defeated Turkey and drew the USA before dispatching [[Colombia national football team|Colombia]] in the semi-final. However, the latter was overshadowed by the sudden on-field collapse of Cameroon midfielder [[Marc-Vivien Foé]] in the 71st minute.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Radnedge|first=Keir|date=27 June 2003|title=Foé dies in Confederations Cup game|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2003/jun/27/newsstory.sport10|access-date=3 December 2022|website=The Guardian|archive-date=4 December 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221204081848/https://www.theguardian.com/football/2003/jun/27/newsstory.sport10|url-status=live}}</ref> Medics spent 45 minutes attempting to restart his heart, and although he was still alive upon arrival at the stadium's medical centre, he died shortly afterwards.<ref>{{Cite web|date=26 June 2003|title=Cameroon star Foe dies|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/3024360.stm|access-date=3 December 2022|website=BBC Sport|archive-date=4 December 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221204081912/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/3024360.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> An autopsy determined the cause of death to have been [[hypertrophic cardiomyopathy]], an hereditary condition known to increase the risk of sudden death during physical exercise.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Molinaro|first=John F.|date=21 May 2009|title=Requiem for a midfielder: Remembering Marc-Vivien Foe|url=https://www.cbc.ca/sports/soccer/requiem-for-a-midfielder-remembering-marc-vivien-foe-1.791439|access-date=3 December 2022|website=CBC|archive-date=4 December 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221204081922/https://www.cbc.ca/sports/soccer/requiem-for-a-midfielder-remembering-marc-vivien-foe-1.791439|url-status=live}}</ref> The Final on June 29 against France, consequently, became not about the game but rather an occasion for both teams and fans to honor Foé. France prevailed 1-0 following [[Thierry Henry]]'s golden goal in the 7th minute of extra time, but abstained from traditional post-match celebrations. Instead, the tournament closed with one last tribute to Foé as Cameroon took a lap around the stadium holding a large photo of their fallen teammate.<ref>{{Cite web|agency=AP|date=1 July 2003|title=Henry bags Cup for France|url=https://www.taipeitimes.com/News/sport/archives/2003/07/01/2003057652|access-date=3 December 2022|website=Taipei Times|archive-date=4 December 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221204081922/https://www.taipeitimes.com/News/sport/archives/2003/07/01/2003057652|url-status=live}}</ref>

In the [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (CAF)|2006 World Cup qualifying round]], Cameroon were drawn into Group 3 with the Ivory Coast, [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]], [[Libya national football team|Libya]], [[Sudan national football team|Sudan]] and [[Benin national football team|Benin]]. Cameroon led the group until their final game, when [[Pierre Womé]] failed to convert a late penalty. On 8 October 2005, Cameroon drew with Egypt 1–1 while eventual World Cup debutants Ivory Coast defeated Sudan 3–1, preventing Cameroon from travelling to [[2006 FIFA World Cup|Germany]].

In Cameroon's [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (CAF)|2010 World Cup qualifying campaign]], the team was grouped with [[Gabon national football team|Gabon]], Togo and Morocco. After a slow start in their campaign with a loss to Togo, the coach of Cameroon, [[Otto Pfister]], resigned. Frenchman [[Paul Le Guen]] was appointed as the new coach after a draw against Morocco. Le Guen's appointment caused an uprise in Cameroon's spirits as they earned a win against Gabon in [[Libreville]], followed by another win against the Panthers four days later in [[Yaoundé]]. One month later, they defeated Togo in Yaoundé by three goals. On 14 November 2009, Cameroon defeated the Atlas Lions of Morocco 2–0 in [[Fez, Morocco|Fez]] in their last match of their campaign. Gabon was also defeated by Togo 1–0 in [[Lomé]]. Both results caused Cameroon to qualify for the [[2010 FIFA World Cup|2010 World Cup]] finals, held in South Africa.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://soccernet.espn.go.com/report?id=262942&league=FIFA.WORLDQ.CAF&cc=5739|title=Indomitable Lions roar through to record sixth finals|date=14 November 2009|work=ESPN|access-date=4 February 2010|archive-date=27 April 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110427083224/http://soccernet.espn.go.com/report?id=262942&league=FIFA.WORLDQ.CAF&cc=5739|url-status=dead}}</ref>

The Indomitable Lions were the first team to be mathematically eliminated in the 2010 World Cup, going out in their second group match to [[Denmark national football team|Denmark]] after losing 1–2, preceded by a 0–1 defeat to [[Japan national football team|Japan]].

[[File:Volker Finke2013.jpg|thumb|200px|Between 2013 and 2015, [[Volker Finke]] was the manager of the national football team of Cameroon.]]
Cameroon started the [[2017 Africa Cup of Nations]] competition with a 1–1 draw to [[Burkina Faso national football team|Burkina Faso]], followed by a 2–1 win against [[Guinea-Bissau national football team|Guinea-Bissau]], and an unconvincing goalless draw against the hosts Gabon. These results were enough for Cameroon to qualify from the group stage to the quarter-finals, where they met Senegal in a close match that Cameroon won 5–4 in a [[Penalty shoot-out (association football)|penalty shootout]] after it had ended goalless in [[Overtime (sports)|extra time]]. In the semi-finals, Cameroon met [[Ghana national football team|Ghana]] and won the match 2–0 to qualify to the final.

On 5 February 2017, and after a close match, Cameroon won the African Cup of Nations for the fifth time after defeating seven-time champions Egypt 2–1 in the [[2017 Africa Cup of Nations Final|final]],<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/38786537|title=Africa Cup of Nations 2017: Cameroon 2-1 Egypt|date=5 February 2017|website=BBC Sport|access-date=30 July 2017|archive-date=10 November 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171110015321/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/38786537|url-status=live}}</ref> by [[Vincent Aboubakar]]'s late goal in the 89th minute of the match.<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.theguardian.com/football/2017/feb/05/africa-cup-nations-final-cameroon-egypt|title= Afcon 2017: Cameroon's Aboubakar wins final with late goal against Egypt|date= 5 February 2017|work= The Guardian|access-date= 12 January 2018|archive-date= 29 May 2019|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20190529234710/https://www.theguardian.com/football/2017/feb/05/africa-cup-nations-final-cameroon-egypt|url-status= live}}</ref>
As champions, Cameroon qualified for the [[2017 FIFA Confederations Cup]] in Russia, where they were eliminated in the group stage.

Cameroon qualified for the [[2022 FIFA World Cup|2022 World Cup]] in Qatar via the [[away goals rule]] after defeating hosts Algeria 2–1 on 29 March 2022 thanks to [[Karl Toko Ekambi]]'s winner in the 124th minute of the second leg of their [[Confederation of African Football|CAF]] [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – CAF third round|third round]] home-and-away tie with The Fennec Foxes.<ref>{{cite web |agency=Reuters |date=29 March 2022 |title=Toko Ekambi scores sensational winner to send Cameroon to World Cup |url=https://www.espn.com/soccer/report?gameId=629214 |access-date=2 December 2022 |website=espn.com |archive-date=3 December 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221203034005/https://www.espn.com/soccer/report?gameId=629214 |url-status=live }}</ref> On 2 December 2022, in the final match of [[2022 FIFA World Cup Group G|Group G]], The Indomitable Lions made history by becoming the first African country to defeat Brazil at the World Cup. Vincent Aboubakar netted the contest's lone goal in the 2nd minute of stoppage time, and subsequently received his second booking and [[Fouls and misconduct (association football)|dismissal]] for removing his shirt during his celebration. It was the Seleção's first group stage loss since a 2–1 defeat to Norway in 1998 and Cameroon's first ever World Cup win since 2002.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Azzoni |first=Tales |date=2 December 2022 |title=Brazil tops Group G despite dramatic loss to Cameroon |url=https://www.thescore.com/s/23792634 |access-date=2 December 2022 |website=The Score}}</ref> Cameroon failed to advance from their group, however, as they finished third behind Brazil and Switzerland, respectively.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Shpigel |first=Ben |date=2 December 2022 |title=A chaotic end to the group stage sends Brazil and Switzerland to the knockout round. |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/02/sports/soccer/cameroon-brazil-world-cup-switzerland.html |access-date=2 December 2022 |website=The New York Times |archive-date=2 December 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221202225745/https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/02/sports/soccer/cameroon-brazil-world-cup-switzerland.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

After the dramatic qualification process for the previous World Cup, the Indomitable Lions of Cameroon had to wait until the last day to secure their ticket for the [[2023 Africa Cup of Nations|AFCON 2023]] (scheduled to be played in January 2024) in [[Ivory Coast]]. Despite a relatively favorable draw for the qualifiers, which included [[Namibia national football team|Namibia]], [[Burundi national football team|Burundi]], and [[Kenya national football team|Kenya]] (the latter eliminated due to administrative interference), the Cameroonian team faced significant challenges. They struggled against the modest Namibian team, managing a 1–1 draw in Yaoundé and then suffering a 2–1 loss in South Africa. After securing a 1–0 victory over Burundi in the first leg, they needed to confirm their qualification in the second leg to avoid missing out on the continent's most prestigious competition, which would have meant watching it at home. In a packed [[Roumdé Adjia Stadium|Roumde Adja stadium]] on Tuesday, September 12, the Indomitable Lions secured their spot in the AFCON 2024 thanks to goals from [[Bryan Mbeumo]] (46'), [[Christopher Wooh]] (59'), and Vincent Aboubakar (90+3').

Cameroon ended up failing to qualify for the [[2026 FIFA World Cup]] after a last-minute goal from [[Chancel Mbemba]] of the DR Congo in the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – CAF second round]], meaning the Indomitable Lions would miss their fourth World Cup since 1982.

==Kits and crests==
{{Commons|Cameroon national football team kits}}
The Cameroon national football team's tradition color is [[green]] shirts, [[red]] shorts and [[yellow]] socks, colors of the [[Flag of Cameroon|national flag]].

===Kit controversies===
Cameroon used sleeveless [[Puma AG|Puma]] shirts at the [[2002 African Cup of Nations]] in Mali, which they won for the fourth time. [[FIFA]], however, did not allow Cameroon to use the same kits as at the 2002 World Cup, and black sleeves were added to the shirts.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/1862872.stm|title=Fifa bans Cameroon shirts|date=9 March 2002|work=BBC Sport|access-date=14 November 2009|archive-date=31 March 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180331111044/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/1862872.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> The [[2004 African Cup of Nations]] witnessed Cameroon again run into controversy regarding their kits. Puma had designed a one-piece kit for the Cameroon team which FIFA declared illegal, stating that the kits must have separate shirts and shorts.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2004-02-07 |title=Kit crisis for Cameroon |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/3468343.stm |access-date=2025-01-09 |work=[[BBC Sport]] |language=en-GB}}</ref> FIFA then imposed fines on Cameroon and deducted six points from their qualifying campaign.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2004-04-16 |title=Fifa hits Cameroon hopes |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/3633409.stm |access-date=2025-01-09 |work=[[BBC Sport]] |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2004-04-17 |title=Cameroon in shirt appeal |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/3634945.stm |access-date=2025-01-09 |work=[[BBC Sport]] |language=en-GB}}</ref> Puma argued that a two-piece kit is not stated as a requirement in the FIFA laws of the game. Puma, however, lost the case in court, and Cameroon were forced to wear two-piece kits, but FIFA subsequently restored the six qualifying points to Cameroon.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2005-04-06 |title=Fifa suffers kit setback |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/4418031.stm |access-date=2025-01-09 |work=[[BBC Sport]] |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2005-10-25 |title=Cameroon kit row resolved |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/4375196.stm |access-date=2025-01-09 |work=[[BBC Sport]] |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Puma and FIFA Settle on One-Piece Kit – DW – 10/26/2005 |url=https://www.dw.com/en/puma-and-fifa-settle-on-one-piece-kit/a-1754165 |access-date=2025-01-09 |website=dw.com |language=en}}</ref>

{{Incomplete list|date=June 2018}}

|-
! Kit supplier
! Period
! Notes
|-
| {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Le Coq Sportif]]
| 1982–1987
|
|-
| {{flagicon|GER}} [[Adidas]]
| 1988–1993
|
|-
| {{flagicon|UK}} [[Mitre Sports International|Mitre]]
| 1993–1995
|
|-
| {{flagicon|ITA}} [[Lotto Sport Italia|Lotto]]
| 1995–1996
|
|-
| {{flagicon|GER}} Adidas
| 1996–1997
|
|-
| {{flagicon|GER}} [[Puma (brand)|Puma]]
| 1998–2018
|
|-
| {{flagicon|FRA}} Le Coq Sportif
| 2019–2022
|
|-
| {{flagicon|USA}} One All Sports
| 2022–2024
|
|-
| {{flagicon|SWI}} Fourteen
| 2025–present
|
|}

== Results and fixtures ==
{{main|Cameroon national football team results (2020–present)}}
The following is a list of match results in the last 12 months, as well as any future matches that have been scheduled.

! Position
! Name
|-
| Head coach
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[David Pagou]]
|-
| Assistant coach
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Martin Ndtoungou Mpile]]
|-
| Goalkeeping coach
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Idriss Carlos Kameni]]
|-
| Video analyst

|-
| Fitness coach
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Bryan Meyeke]]
|-
| Doctor

|-
| Physiotherapist
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Daniel Che Awah]]
|-
| Physiotherapist
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Elias Kaleguem Fomekong]]
|-
| General Coordinator of National teams 
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Benoit Christian Angbwa]]
|-
| Assistant coordinator 1
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Serge Reinold Pensy]]
|-
| Assistant coordinator 2 
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Sarah Ntui]]
|-
| Team manager

|-
| Team Media officer
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Elie Thierry Ndoh]]
|-
| Liaison officer
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Arnold Ebolo Abada]]
|-
| Technical director
| {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Engelbert Janvier Mbarga Ondoa]]
|}

===Coaching history===
{{div col|colwidth=25em}}
* Technical Committee (1960–1965)
* {{flagicon|FRA}} [[Dominique Colonna]] (1965–1970)
* {{flagicon|CMR|1961}} {{ill|Raymond Fobete|fr|Raymond Fobete}} (1970)
* {{flagicon|FRG}} [[Peter Schnittger]] (1970–1973)
* {{flagicon|YUG}} [[Vladimir Beara]] (1973–1975)
* {{flagicon|YUG}} [[Ivan Ridanović]] (1976–1979)
* {{flagicon|YUG}} [[Branko Žutić]] (1980–1982)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Jean Vincent]] (1982)
* {{flagicon|YUG}} [[Radivoje Ognjanović]] (1982–1984)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Claude Le Roy]] (1985–1988, 1998)
* {{flagicon|URS}} [[Valery Nepomnyashchy]] (1988–1990)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Philippe Redon]] (1990–1993)
* {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Jean Manga-Onguéné]] (1993–1994, 1997–1998)
* {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Léonard Nseké]] (1994)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Henri Michel]] (1994)
* {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Jules Nyongha]] (1994–1996, 2007)
* {{flagicon|BEL}} [[Henri Depireux]] (1996–1997)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Pierre Lechantre]] (1998–2001)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Robert Corfou]] (2001)
* {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Jean-Paul Akono]] (2001, 2012–2013)
* {{flagicon|GER}} [[Winfried Schäfer]] (2001–2004)
* {{flagicon|POR}} [[Artur Jorge (footballer, born 1946)|Artur Jorge]] (2004–2006)
* {{flagicon|NED}} [[Arie Haan]] (2006–2007)
* {{flagicon|GER}} [[Otto Pfister]] (2007–2009)
* {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Thomas N'Kono]] (2009)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Paul Le Guen]] (2009–2010)
* {{flagicon|ESP}} [[Javier Clemente]] (2010–2011)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Denis Lavagne]] (2011–2012)
* {{flagicon|GER}} [[Volker Finke]] (2013–2015)
* {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Alexandre Belinga]] (2015–2016)
* {{flagicon|BEL}} [[Hugo Broos]] (2016–2017)
* {{flagicon|CMR}} [[Rigobert Song]] (2017–2018, 2022–2024)
* {{flagicon|NED}} [[Clarence Seedorf]] (2018–2019)
* {{flagicon|POR}} [[António Oliveira (footballer, born 1961)|Toni Conceição]] (2019–2022)
* {{flagicon|BEL}} [[Marc Brys]] (2024–2025)
* {{flagicon|CMR}} [[David Pagou]] (2025–present)
{{div col end}}

==Players==

===Current squad===
The following players were selected for the [[2026_FIFA_Series_(men%27s_matches)#Australia|2026 FIFA series]] in March 2026.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.afrik-foot.com/cameroun-11-absents-de-la-can-pour-les-fifa-series-david-pagou-liste|title= Cameroun : plus de 10 joueurs de la CAN laissés de côté pour les FIFA Series, David Pagou fait tourner !|date=19 March 2026|website=afrik-foot.com}}</ref>
<br>''Caps and goals correct as of 31 March 2026, after the match against [[China national soccer team|China]].''
 
{{nat fs g start}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Devis Epassy]]|age={{bda|1993|2|2|df=y}}|caps=16|goals=0|club=[[FC Dinamo București|Dinamo București]]|clubnat=ROU}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Simon Ngapandouetnbu]]|age={{bda|2003|4|12|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Montpellier HSC|Montpellier]]|clubnat=FRA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Blondy Nna Noukeu]]|age={{bda|2001|11|17|df=y}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[US Boulogne|Boulogne]]|clubnat=FRA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Housseini Ousmanou]]|age={{bda|2002|12|28|df=y}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[Gazelle FA|Gazelle]]|clubnat=CMR}}
{{nat fs break}} 
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Jackson Tchatchoua]]|age={{bda|2001|9|14|df=y}}|caps=15|goals=0|club=[[Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.|Wolverhampton Wanderers]]|clubnat=ENG}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Darlin Yongwa]]|age={{bda|2000|9|21|df=y}}|caps=14|goals=1|club=[[FC Lorient|Lorient]]|clubnat=FRA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Aboubakar Nagida]]|age={{bda|2005|6|28|df=y}}|caps=10|goals=0|club=[[Stade Rennais FC|Rennes]]|clubnat=FRA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Che Malone]]|age={{bda|1999|5|23|df=y}}|caps=8|goals=0|club=[[USM Alger]]|clubnat=ALG}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Samuel Kotto]]|age={{bda|2003|9|3|df=y}}|caps=5|goals=0|club=[[Stade de Reims|Reims]]|clubnat=FRA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Oliver Kamdem]]|age={{bda|2002|10|15|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[PFC Levski Sofia|Levski Sofia]]|clubnat=BUL}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Kévin Keben]]|age={{bda|2004|1|26|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Watford F.C.|Watford]]|clubnat=ENG}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Stephane Keller]]|age={{bda|2001|8|20|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Al-Ittihad Club (Jeddah)|Al-Ittihad]]|clubnat=KSA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Emmanuel Moungam]]|age={{bda|2006|4|26|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Landskrona BoIS|Landskrona]]|clubnat=SWE}}

{{nat fs end}}

===Recent call-ups===
The following players have also been called up for the team in the last twelve months and are still available for selection.
<!--Sorted by position, most recent call-up, caps, goals and last name.-->
{{nat fs r start}}

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Loïc Etoga]]|age={{bda|2003|4|1|df=y}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[FC Villefranche Beaujolais|Villefranche]]|clubnat=FRA|latest=v. {{fb|CPV}}, 9 September 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Bryan Mbeumo]]|other=[[Captain (association football)|third captain]]|age={{bda|1999|8|7|df=y}}|caps=32|goals=7|club=[[Manchester United F.C.|Manchester United]]|clubnat=ENG|latest=v. {{fb|AUS}}, 27 March 2026 <sup>INJ</sup>}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Christian Bassogog]]|other=[[Captain (association football)|captain]]|age={{bda|1995|10|18|df=y}}|caps=59|goals=8|club=[[Al-Okhdood Club|Al-Okhdood]]|clubnat=KSA|latest=[[2025 Africa Cup of Nations]]}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Georges-Kévin Nkoudou]]|age={{bda|1995|2|14|df=y}}|caps=20|goals=3|club=[[Diriyah Club|Diriyah]]|clubnat=KSA|latest=[[2025 Africa Cup of Nations]]}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Frank Magri]]|age={{bda|1999|9|4|df=y}}|caps=20|goals=2|club=[[Toulouse FC|Toulouse]]|clubnat=FRA|latest=[[2025 Africa Cup of Nations]]}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Patrick Soko]]|age={{bda|1997|10|31|df=y}}|caps=5|goals=1|club=[[UD Almería|Almería]]|clubnat=ESP|latest=[[2025 Africa Cup of Nations]]}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Vincent Aboubakar]]|other=[[Captain (association football)|captain]]|age={{bda|1992|1|22|df=y}}|caps=117|goals=45|club=[[Free agent|''Unattached'']]|clubnat=|latest=v. {{fb|DRC}}, 13 November 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Eric Maxim Choupo-Moting]]|age={{bda|1989|3|23|df=y}}|caps=76|goals=20|club=[[New York Red Bulls]]|clubnat=USA|latest=v. {{fb|COD}}, 13 November 2025 <sup>INJ</sup>}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Moumi Ngamaleu]]|age={{bda|1994|7|9|df=y}}|caps=64|goals=5|club=[[FC Dynamo Moscow|Dynamo Moscow]]|clubnat=RUS |latest=v. {{fb|DRC}}, 13 November 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Tidjani Ahmadou Abdoullahi]]|age={{bda|2007|7|19|df=y}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[Player FC Yaoundé|Player Yaoundé]]|clubnat=CMR|latest=v. {{fb|CPV}}, 9 September 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
<sup>INJ</sup><span style="font-size:90%"> = Withdrew from the squad due to injury</span><br />
<sup>SUS</sup><span style="font-size:90%"> = Serving suspension</span><br />
<sup>PRE</sup><span style="font-size:90%"> = Preliminary squad / standby</span><br />
<sup>WD</sup><span style="font-size:90%"> = Withdrew from the squad</span>
{{nat fs end}}

== Player records ==
{{updated|13 November 2025}}<ref name="rsssf senegal players">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/kam-recintlp.html |title=Cameroon&nbsp;– Record International Players |author=Mamrud, Roberto |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=28 June 2018 |archive-date=10 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220710163232/https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/kam-recintlp.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Cameroon.''

===Most appearances===
[[File:Rigobert Song 2008.jpg|thumb|200px|Rigobert Song is Cameroon's most capped player with 137 appearances.]]

{| class="wikitable sortable" style="text-align:center;"
|-
!width=30px|Rank
! style="width:175px;"|Player
!width=50px|Caps
!width=50px|Goals
! style="width:100px;"|Career
|-
| 1 || align=left| [[Rigobert Song]] || 137 || 5 || {{nowrap|1993–2010}}
|-
| rowspan="2" |2 ||align=left| [[Samuel Eto'o]] || 118 || 56 || 1997–2014
|-
| align=left| [[Geremi Njitap]] || 118 || 13 || 1996–2010
|-
| 4 || align=left| '''[[Vincent Aboubakar]]''' || 117 || 45 || 2010–present
|-
| 5 || align=left| [[Emmanuel Kundé]] || 96 || 16 || 1979–1992
|-
| 6 || align=left| [[Nicolas Nkoulou]] || 83 || 2 || 2008–2023
|-
| 7 ||align=left| [[Jacques Songo'o]] || 80 || 0 || 1983–2002
|-
| 8 ||align=left| [[Roger Milla]] || 77 || 43 || 1973–1994
|-
| 9 || align=left| '''[[Eric Maxim Choupo-Moting]]''' || 76 || 20 || 2010–present
|-
| rowspan="2"|10 || align=left| [[Carlos Kameni]] || 73 || 0 || 2001–2019
|-
|align=left|[[François Omam-Biyik]] || 73 || 26 || 1985–1998
|}

===Top goalscorers===
[[File:Samuel Eto'o.jpg|thumb|175px|Samuel Eto'o is Cameroon's all-time top goalscorer with 56 goals.]]

{| class="wikitable sortable" style="text-align:center;"
|-
!width=30px|Rank
! style="width:175px;"|Player
!width=50px|Goals
!width=50px|Caps

|-

|-

|-

|-

|-
| 9 ||align=left| [[Emmanuel Kundé]] || 16 || 96 || {{#expr:16/96 round 2}} || 1979–1992
|-
| 10 ||align=left| [[André Kana-Biyik]] || 15 || 59 || {{#expr:15/59 round 2}} || 1985–1994
|}

:''*Denotes draws including knockout matches decided via [[Penalty shoot-out (association football)|penalty shoot-out]].''
:''**Red border colour indicates tournament was held on home soil.''

{| class="wikitable collapsible collapsed" style="width:50%;text-align:center"
|-
!style="background:#006400;{{box-shadow border|a|red|2px}};color:yellow" colspan=2| Cameroon's Africa Cup of Nations record
|-
!First match
|{{fb|CMR}} 3–2 {{fb-rt|CIV}}<br />(6 February 1970; [[Khartoum]], Sudan)
|-
!Biggest win
|{{fb|CMR}} 5–1 {{fb-rt|ZAM}}<br />(26 January 2008; [[Kumasi]], Ghana)
|-
!Biggest defeat
|{{fb|RSA}} 3–0 {{fb-rt|CMR}}<br />(13 January 1996; [[Johannesburg]], South Africa)
|-
!Best result
|'''Champions''' in [[1984 African Cup of Nations|1984]], [[1988 African Cup of Nations|1988]], [[2000 African Cup of Nations|2000]], [[2002 African Cup of Nations|2002]], [[2017 Africa Cup of Nations|2017]]
|-
!Worst result
|Group stage in [[1970 African Cup of Nations|1970]], [[1982 African Cup of Nations|1982]], [[1990 African Cup of Nations|1990]], [[1996 African Cup of Nations|1996]], [[2015 Africa Cup of Nations|2015]]
|}

===African Nations Championship===
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;"
|-
!colspan=9|[[African Nations Championship]] record
|-
!colspan=9|Appearances: 4
|-

!Round
!Position
!{{Tooltip|Pld|Games played}}
!{{Tooltip|W|Won}}
!{{Tooltip|D|Drawn}}*
!{{Tooltip|L|Lost}}

|-
|{{flagicon|Ivory Coast}} [[2009 African Nations Championship|2009]]||colspan="8"|''Did not qualify''
|-

|-

|-
!'''Total'''||Round 1||1/19||3||1||0||2||3||5
|}
:''Football at the Summer Olympics has been an under-23 tournament since 1992.''

|{{flagicon|Algeria}} [[Football at the 1978 All-Africa Games|1978]]|||5th||3||0||2||1||2||3
|-

|-

|}

==Honours==
===Global===
* '''[[FIFA Confederations Cup]]'''
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[2003 FIFA Confederations Cup|2003]]

===Intercontinental===
* '''[[Afro-Asian Cup of Nations]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (1)''': [[1985 Afro-Asian Cup of Nations|1985]]

===Continental===
* '''[[Africa Cup of Nations|CAF African Cup of Nations]]'''
** [[File:Coppa Africa.svg|22px]] '''Champions (5)''': [[1984 African Cup of Nations|1984]], [[1988 African Cup of Nations|1988]],  [[2000 African Cup of Nations|2000]], [[2002 African Cup of Nations|2002]], [[2017 Africa Cup of Nations|2017]]
** [[File:Silver medal africa.svg|15px]] Runners-up (2):  [[1986 African Cup of Nations|1986]], [[2008 Africa Cup of Nations|2008]]
** [[File:Bronze medal africa.svg|15px]] Third place (2): [[1972 African Cup of Nations|1972]], [[2021 Africa Cup of Nations|2021]]

===Subregional===
* '''[[UDEAC Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (4)''': [[UDEAC Cup#1984 UDEAC Cup|1984]], [[UDEAC Cup#1986 UDEAC Cup|1986]], [[UDEAC Cup#1987 UDEAC Cup|1987]], [[UDEAC Cup#1989 UDEAC Cup|1989]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (2):  [[UDEAC Cup#1988 UDEAC Cup|1988]], [[UDEAC Cup#1990 UDEAC Cup|1990]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[UDEAC Cup#1985 UDEAC Cup|1985]]
* '''[[CEMAC Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (3)''': [[2003 CEMAC Cup|2003]], [[2005 CEMAC Cup|2005]], [[2008 CEMAC Cup|2008]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (2):  [[2006 CEMAC Cup|2006]], [[2010 CEMAC Cup|2010]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[2014 CEMAC Cup|2014]]
* '''[[Football at the Central African Games|Central African Games]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Gold medal (2)''': 1976, [[Football at the 1987 Central African Games|1987]]

===Friendly===
* '''Jeux de la Coupe des Tropiques (1)''': 1964
* '''[[LG Cup (association football)|LG Cup]] (1)''': [[2011 LG Cup (Morocco)|2011]]
* '''[[1994 Greece Tournament|Greece Tournament]] (1)''': 1994

===Awards===
* '''[[CAF Awards#African National Team of the Year|African National Team of the Year]] (7)''': 1984, 1987, 1988, 1989, 1990, 2000, 2003

===Summary===
{| class="wikitable" width=30% style="font-size:90%; text-align:center;"
|-
!Competition !!{{gold1}} !!{{silver2}} !!{{bronze3}} !!Total
|-
|align=left|[[FIFA Confederations Cup]]
|0||1||0||1
|-
|align=left|[[Africa Cup of Nations|CAF African Cup of Nations]]
|5||2||2||9
|-
|align=left|[[Afro-Asian Cup of Nations]]
|1||0||0||1
|-
!Total!!6!!3!!2!!11
|}
